name: Beatles Paul Is Dead
description: The 'Paul Is Dead' conspiracy theory, originating in 1969, posits that Paul McCartney of The Beatles died in 1966 and was secretly replaced by a look-alike. Proponents claim that the remaining Beatles concealed his death to avoid mass hysteria and maintain their commercial success. The theory is based on alleged clues found in Beatles album artwork, lyrics, and promotional materials. These clues are interpreted as symbolic representations of McCartney's death and replacement. For example, the Abbey Road album cover, with the Beatles walking across a zebra crossing, is interpreted as a funeral procession. Lennon, dressed in white, represents the clergyman; Starr, in black, is the undertaker; Harrison, in denim, is the gravedigger; and McCartney, barefoot and out of step, is the corpse. Backmasked messages in songs are also cited as evidence. Mainstream perspectives dismiss the theory as a hoax or a product of overinterpretation and pareidolia (seeing patterns in random information). Academics and biographers point to McCartney's continued musical output and public appearances as definitive proof of his survival. They argue that the 'clues' are either coincidences, intentional red herrings planted by the Beatles themselves as a joke, or simply misinterpretations of artistic expression. The theory gained widespread attention through college newspapers and radio stations in the United States and quickly spread internationally. Despite its lack of factual basis, the 'Paul Is Dead' conspiracy theory remains a popular topic of discussion and speculation, demonstrating the power of rumor and the human tendency to seek hidden meanings.
subtitle: A Conspiracy Theory About a Beatle

claims.claim: Paul McCartney died in 1966 and was replaced by a look-alike.
claims.evidence: Alleged clues in Beatles albums and songs.
claims.criticism: McCartney's continued existence and public appearances.

claims.claim: The Abbey Road album cover depicts a funeral procession for McCartney.
claims.evidence: The Beatles' clothing and poses are interpreted as symbolic representations of a funeral.
claims.criticism: Artistic interpretation open to subjective interpretation; no verifiable evidence.

claims.claim: Backmasked messages in Beatles songs reveal the truth about McCartney's death.
claims.evidence: Recordings played backwards allegedly contain messages confirming McCartney's death.
claims.criticism: Backmasking is prone to interpretation and is often unintentional; these 'messages' are highly subjective.

keyFigures.name: Fred LaBour
keyFigures.role: Author of the 'Michigan Daily' article that popularized the theory

keyFigures.name: Joel Glazier
keyFigures.role: Promoter of the theory through radio broadcasts

keyFigures.name: Paul McCartney
keyFigures.role: The subject of the conspiracy, repeatedly disproving it through continued activity.

mythologyConnections: Orpheus myth (descent into the underworld and return)

timeline.event: Rumored date of Paul McCartney's alleged death.
Publication of articles in US college newspapers popularizing the conspiracy theory.
Continued speculation and analysis of Beatles' work for 'clues'.

sources.author: Fred LaBour
sources.work: McCartney Dead; New Evidence Brought to Light
sources.title: Fred LaBour

sources.author: Andru Reeve
sources.work: Turn Me On, Dead Man: The Beatles and the 'Paul Is Dead' Hoax
sources.title: Andru Reeve
